# Transcrição: 05-Landing Page Design Example

- **Fonte:** 05-Landing Page Design Example
- **Duração:** 08:19
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:04]

What I want to show you here is the process I go through to analyze my landing pages or key pages to find opportunities for optimization. So in the other videos we've looked at, looking at other sites for inspiration, looking at site speed, etc. etc. But that's all looking at externally outside of your landing page. The first thing is you can always look at your own landing page and critique it. You can pass it to friends and say what you think etc. But when you've got traffic coming into your lander, that's the time to start analyzing what's working and what's not. So in this example here we use a tool called Hotjar. There's also Lucky Orange, there's many other tools out there as well that allow you to track where people are scrolling on your page, where they're clicking and any kind of interactions they're having outside of that as well. To give an example on this particular landing page there is a banner when you land on the page and what I find quite interesting is I'm looking at the click map. Now because this headline is underlined, the user, there's only two clicks, but the user thinks that this is a clickable element. Now bear in mind this particular test is set up to capture a percentage of visitors to this landing page. So not 100% of visitors. So you have to extrapolate and say right I'm only tracking 10% of people, so if I factor it up by a factor of 10 that's how many people in this date period have clicks for example. So I'm looking at this and thinking yeah maybe removing the underlining is probably going to be a better thing for us to make sure that people are not clicking because it's not clickable. You might look at that and say well I don't care, they clicked it, nothing happens. But if there's something I want you to take away from conversion rate optimization is that you need to make your funnel slick and distraction free. Like this is now all about data and science. It's all about getting your user into your key action as fast as possible, but in the best way possible. So if we've talked about Facebook advertising and finding the best ads and audiences and building the avatars and things like that, don't waste the traffic that you're getting. So you can be the master of media buying and traffic. If you are not mastering conversion rate optimization, are you really a media buyer? I would argue not. And you know media buyer isn't just firing up traffic into the page and just hoping to see if it works. A true media buyer and someone that really is trying to master Facebook ads is balancing traffic and conversion at the same time and looking at average order value and the costs and lifetime value as well. But fundamentally when you're traffic driving from any ad platform, you need to look at quality of traffic and quality of conversion. So although these may seem like small issues, that could be one or two sales or people that we've lost out on because they're frustrated. And you know what I'll do is I'll share through the page some other examples of people clicking

## [03:04 - 06:05]

where it's not clickable. It's not to say that you know you change this it's going to massively improve conversion rate, but you need to make a list of priority items that you are going to test and then test them to see if they make a difference. Now we could run this as a real fast split test and take out the underlying and measure it over a period of a week, two weeks depending on how much traffic we're getting and see if it makes a difference. Who knows maybe conversion rate goes up by 0.1% and that results in higher profitability, return on ad spend, etc. Maybe it has zero impact, but the fact is as you go down your analysis, you want to find opportunities and mark them down. So you know I would mark this down as a potential test on the landing page, remove the underlying from the headline and use a kind of theory and hypothesis basis. So the way I would structure it is you know I think by removing the underlying from the headline on the landing page, users will be more focused and therefore potentially convert at a high rate. So that becomes a test and then you grade that based on impact, cost and effort and then decide what to test next. And it's a real simple process, but it is a process like the whole BPM method is a process as well. So let's go through this page. There's a button here and you can see there's a lot more clicks here. That's great. There's some random clicks here. It might just be noise, so I'll ignore that for now. And then I kind of go down here and an interesting thing here is the images are also getting clicked as well. Now the images on the landing page are not clickable, but when I saw this I thought maybe people are looking to zoom in on the image. So if you're on a mobile device, if you're on a desktop as well, you might want to zoom in and find, look at a bit more detail. It's an expected journey when you're buying products to be able to zoom in and look at images in more detail. So the standard Ecom store would have images. You click the image, you can zoom in, have a look. They do it on Amazon for example. So one thing to bear in mind is when you are designing your landing page is making it as normal as possible. The more you kind of move away from standard practices, the more friction you're introducing. So this landing page works extremely well, but there are small elements that as I'm kind of going down, I'm kind of looking at and thinking, right, what is the user thinking here? Now this data is quite light at the moment. We've only just recently started for this store, but they're the things that I'm going to be tracking and monitoring. Why are people clicking here, for example, we scroll down and we kind of look at the product base. And what's interesting here is at least based on the data that we've got so far, and you know, if this was more substantial, I'd probably take more into account. There's only one click, one click recorded. So there's not enough data at this point to give me enough confidence as to what's going on. But if for example, we've been running this for a month, we've got lots of data, and this was not getting clicked. I would look back at my sales data and look at kind of what's being bought.

## [06:05 - 08:19]

Now I know, for example, most people are buying the three set. So that's great. But there are lots of people buying this as well. And there's a few points behind this. Now you can't see the pricing here. It's just part of hot jar and they kind of mark out any numbers based on security and bank details and stuff. For security reasons, they cannot share. But the price differences are based on here's the base price. This is 20% off. This is 30% off. This seems like the better deal. So getting three with a percent off. A lot of people are falling into that and our average order values reflect that as well. But for those are not confident yet, they want to buy one and give it a go first. What I would then do is in my post purchase remarketing. So whether it's through ads or email is for those that have bought just one item, try and see if they'd upsell into two or three or four based on their experience. Because I know if people are convinced by this product and read through it, then if they're buying two or three, they really want to give it a chance. They then they're confident it could make a difference to their lighting, for example, for this product. So they're going all out and going for two or three. That's great. But for those that just go for one, either they only need one or they are just testing the waters. Think about how you position that post purchase as well. So it's not to say you absolutely have to funnel everyone into three items. It's part of a bundle deal. For example, when they come on this page, it's understanding that there are different people out there. So someone might buy this, they like the products, they want to give it a go first before they consider more. And that then makes a difference what you do post purchase as well. So that's just giving an idea of how I kind of look at my product page and find opportunities. I install this on literally every landing page that I traffic. We either install hot jar or lucky orange. This is part of really optimizing and tweaking performance because like I keep saying, the better you optimize at this stage, the better your scalability will be in terms of stability and profitability. So this is a big part of optimizing that experience is to look at your landing page. Look at the click map. You can look at the heat map, find out what people are doing and what you can do to improve it.
